sections.title: Restrictions on co-branding and solicitation of insurance business
sections.summary: This section prohibits licensed insurers from co-branding with other entities without prior written consent from the Authority. It also restricts solicitation of insurance business outside Singapore for insurers, requiring that any solicitation must be limited to Singapore operations and not extend to foreign branches or head offices. Violations incur fines and imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border co-branding and solicitation activities for insurers, requiring Singapore-specific advertising and business operations to prevent unauthorized international data transfers.

requirements.text: A licensed insurer must not use, together with its name, logo or trade mark in Singapore, the name, logo or trade mark of any person who carries on risk assumption business

A licensed insurer must not use, together with its name, logo or trade mark in Singapore, the name, logo or trade mark of any person who is not a licensed insurer, authorized reinsurer or foreign insurer

A person must not solicit insurance business for any insurer other than licensed insurers, authorized reinsurers, foreign insurers under foreign insurer schemes, or other insurers entitled to carry on insurance business in Singapore
Solicitation must only be for the insurance business in Singapore of the insurer
Solicitation must not be for foreign branches or head offices of the insurer

sections.source_quote: Any person who contravenes subsection (1), (2) or (5) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction — (a) in the case of an individual, to a fine not exceeding $12,500 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months or to both and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $1,250 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction; or (b) in any other case, to a fine not exceeding $25,000 and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $2,500 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction.
sections.relevance_reason: Restricts third-party co-branding and solicitation within Singapore

sections.title: Licensing of insurers and representative offices
sections.summary: This section outlines the licensing requirements for insurers operating in Singapore, including application procedures, financial criteria, and registration of representative offices. It mandates that representative offices must be registered with the Authority and that insurers must comply with financial requirements and conditions to maintain their licenses. Non-compliance results in fines and potential license revocation.

sections.title: Definitions of related entities and relationships
sections.summary: This section defines specific relationships and entities used throughout the Insurance Act. It establishes criteria for when an entity (A) is considered related to another entity (B), including familial relationships, corporate control structures, and contractual arrangements. These definitions apply to insurers and their relationships with policyholders, other entities, and the regulatory authority. The definitions are critical for determining the scope of insurance operations and regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines key relationships that impact data transfer assessments by clarifying when entities are considered related for regulatory purposes, which affects cross-border data sharing and processing obligations.

sections.title: Objection to existing control of licensed insurers incorporated in Singapore
sections.summary: The Authority may issue a written notice of objection to individuals or entities controlling licensed insurers in Singapore if they fail to comply with conditions from section 26 or 27, provide false information, cease to be fit and proper, or pose risks to prudent business conduct. The notice requires written representations within a specified timeframe before the Authority decides on objection. If objected, the person must take steps to cease control or comply with directions within a reasonable period.

sections.title: Restriction on unsecured loans to directors and employees of insurers
sections.summary: A licensed insurer in Singapore must not grant unsecured loans or advances to directors or employees exceeding $5,000 in aggregate at any one time, or one year's emolument for employees. The definition of 'director' includes immediate family members. This restriction applies to all licensed insurers conducting business in Singapore.

sections.title: Authorisation of reinsurance businesses outside Singapore
sections.summary: Reinsurers operating outside Singapore must apply to the Authority for authorisation to conduct reinsurance business in Singapore. The Authority may impose conditions, vary authorisation, or revoke authorisation based on specific grounds including failure to meet financial obligations, misconduct, or public interest considerations. Reinsurers must maintain reinsurance deposits for each class of business.

sections.title: Reinsurer deposit requirements for Singapore financial regulators
sections.summary: This section mandates that deposits for reinsurers must comply with prescribed forms, assets, and methods as directed by the Authority. All income from such deposits must be paid to the reinsurer making the deposit. The Authority may prescribe rights, obligations, and incidental matters related to deposits. Banks can provide deposits via covenants if they meet the Authority's requirements, and these deposits are treated as if made directly by the reinsurer. The Authority may also require reinsurers to maintain minimum Singapore-based assets to meet liabilities, specify asset types and minimum amounts, and impose trustee requirements for asset custody. Failure to comply with asset maintenance directives results in fines up to $250,000 per day.
sections.dtia_summary: Reinsurers must maintain Singapore-based assets to meet liabilities, with specific asset types and minimum amounts prescribed by the Authority. This affects data storage and financial stability requirements for cross-border insurance operations.

sections.title: Insurance intermediaries must disclose material information to policyholders
sections.summary: Insurance intermediaries must disclose all material information to individuals before inviting them to enter into insurance contracts. This includes the licensed insurer's name, the intermediary's relationship with the insurer, the premium charged, and any other information specified by the Authority. The requirement applies to both individual policies and group policies where individuals are liable for premiums. Violations are punishable by fines up to $25,000 or imprisonment for up to 12 months.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific disclosures for insurance intermediaries, requiring transparency about the insurer, premium costs, and policy terms before data sharing with policyholders. It impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring clear information about the insurer and intermediary relationships.

sections.title: Insurance brokers must maintain separate bank accounts for client funds
sections.summary: Every registered insurance broker in Singapore must establish and maintain a separate bank account with a licensed bank for all client-related funds. The account must be specific to each type of insurance business if the broker operates multiple types. The Authority may prescribe rules for the types of money, payment methods, investment strategies, and distribution of funds held in these accounts. Any lien or charge on these funds is void unless it relates to fees owed to the broker.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires insurance brokers to segregate client funds in licensed bank accounts, which impacts data transfer DTIAs by mandating financial isolation of client data across borders. The requirement for separate accounts per business type affects cross-border data handling and financial transparency.

sections.title: Parent supervisory authority inspections of Singapore insurers
sections.summary: A parent supervisory authority may inspect Singapore-based licensed insurers' books with prior written approval from the Authority, provided the inspection serves supervisory functions, the authority ensures confidentiality, and complies with conditions. The insurer must grant access to books and information but may limit access to avoid disrupting normal operations. Refusal to comply results in fines up to $100,000.

sections.source_quote: A parent supervisory authority may, with the prior written approval of the Authority and under conditions of secrecy, conduct an inspection in Singapore of the books of the licensed insurer in Singapore in accordance with this section, if the following conditions are satisfied: (a) the inspection is required by the parent supervisory authority for the sole purpose of carrying out its supervisory functions; (b) the parent supervisory authority — (i) is prohibited by the laws applicable to the parent supervisory authority from disclosing information obtained by it in the course of the inspection to any other person; or (ii) has given to the Authority such written undertaking as to the confidentiality of the information obtained, as the Authority may determine; and (c) the parent supervisory authority has given a written undertaking to the Authority to comply with the provisions of this Act and such conditions as the Authority may impose under subsection (2).

sections.title: Examination procedures for regulatory investigations
sections.summary: The Authority may require individuals to provide assistance and appear for examination during investigations. The examinee must answer relevant questions on oath or affirmation, and the examination must occur privately with restricted attendance. The investigator may record statements, provide copies to the examinee, and share records with legal representatives under specific conditions. Copies must be handled with restrictions to prevent misuse in legal proceedings.

These procedures impact data handling during cross-border investigations but are not directly relevant to data transfer impact assessments.

sections.title: Nomination revocation and death benefit distribution rules for insurance policies
sections.summary: This section outlines rules for revoking nominations and distributing death benefits when nominees die. If a nominee dies before the policy owner, the nomination is deemed revoked if no surviving nominee exists. If one surviving nominee exists, their portion is increased by the deceased nominee's share. If multiple survivors exist, each receives a proportional share of the deceased nominee's portion. In cases of uncertain survival, deaths are presumed to occur in order of seniority. The section also specifies when nominations are deemed revoked, such as when the policy owner assigns the policy, makes a will, or creates a new nomination. Death benefits are distributed according to the last valid nomination, the last will, or intestate succession rules as appropriate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects data transfer impact assessments by defining how death benefit distributions are handled across jurisdictions when nominees die, requiring careful assessment of nomination validity and distribution mechanisms in cross-border insurance contexts.
